#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::StandardScaler(with_mean=true, with_std=true) Standardize features by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance. The standard score of a sample x is calculated as: z=x−μσz = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}z=σx−μ​ where μ\muμ is the mean of the training samples and σ\sigmaσ is the standard deviation. Centering and scaling happen independently on each feature by computing the relevant statistics on the samples in the training set. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler. Parameters: with_mean : bool, default=true If true, center the data before scaling (bool, default true). with_std : bool, default=true If true, scale the data to unit variance (bool, default true). Attributes: with_mean : bool Whether centering is enabled. with_std : bool Whether scaling to unit variance is enabled. mean : RowVectorType Per-feature mean (1 × n_features). var : RowVectorType Per-feature variance (1 × n_features). scale : RowVectorType Per-feature standard deviation (1 × n_features). n_samples_seen : IndexType Number of samples processed during fit().
Compute the mean and std to be used for later scaling. Parameters: X Training data of shape (n_samples, n_features). Returns: result Reference to the fitted transformer (*this).

Online update of the mean and variance using Chan's parallel algorithm (a numerically-stable Welford variant). partial_fit(X1).partial_fit(X2) produces the same fitted attributes (within floating-point tolerance) as fit([X1; X2]), matching sklearn's StandardScaler.partial_fit contract. Attributes: nu : Scalar kernel : Kernel classes : const Eigen::VectorXi support : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & n_support : int intercept : Scalar
Raw decision function — sum_s dual_coef_s K(x, sv_s) + b.

#include <Skigen/SVM> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::NuSVR(nu=0.5, C=1.0, kernel=Kernel::RBF, degree=3, gamma=0, coef0=0, tol=1e-3, max_iter=1000, random_state=std::nullopt) Nu-Support Vector Regression with kernels. In nu-SVR, nu controls the fraction of support vectors and bounds the fraction of points outside the epsilon tube; the tube width epsilon is learned from the data rather than fixed. Skigen fits the dual coefficients with the same sub-gradient solver as SVR, choosing epsilon as the (1 - nu) quantile of the absolute residuals so that roughly a nu fraction of training points lie outside the tube. Mirrors the dense core of sklearn.svm.NuSVR. Attributes: nu : Scalar C : Scalar kernel : Kernel epsilon_fitted : Scalar support : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & n_support : int

#include <Skigen/SVM> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::OneClassSVM(kernel=Kernel::RBF, degree=3, gamma=0, coef0=0, nu=0.5, tol=1e-3, max_passes=50, random_state=std::nullopt) Unsupervised outlier detection with a one-class SVM. Solves the one-class nu-SVM dual with a dedicated SMO variant and exposes sklearn-style scoring: decision_function, score_samples, predict (+1 inlier, -1 outlier), and offset_. Mirrors the dense core of sklearn.svm.OneClassSVM. Attributes: kernel : Kernel gamma : Scalar nu : Scalar support : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & n_support : int dual_coef : VectorType offset : Scalar
Signed distance to the separating hyperplane (rho-shifted).
Unshifted kernel score for each sample.
Predict +1 for inliers and -1 for outliers.

Eigen::MatrixXd X(12, 2); for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) { X(i, 0) = 0.2 * std::sin(static_cast<double>(i)); X(i, 1) = 0.2 * std::cos(static_cast<double>(i)); } X(10, 0) = 6.0; X(10, 1) = 6.0; X(11, 0) = -6.0; X(11, 1) = 5.0; using K = Skigen::OneClassSVM<double>::Kernel; Skigen::OneClassSVM<double> det(K::RBF, 3, 0.0, 0.0, /*nu=*/0.2); det.fit(X); const Eigen::VectorXi labels = det.predict(X);

#include <Skigen/SVM> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::SVC(C=1.0, kernel=Kernel::RBF, degree=3, gamma=0, coef0=0, probability=false, tol=1e-3, max_passes=50, random_state=std::nullopt) C-Support Vector Classification with kernels. Mirrors sklearn.svm.SVC for the binary case (multiclass via one-vs-one as in libsvm). When probability=true, Platt scaling is fitted via internal 5-fold cross-validation on the decision function scores, providing calibrated posterior probabilities through predict_proba(). Attributes: C : Scalar kernel : Kernel gamma : Scalar classes : const Eigen::VectorXi n_classes : int support : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & n_support : int
Fit from a sparse design matrix (densifies internally).
Predict from a sparse design matrix (densifies internally).
Raw decision function — sum_s alpha_s y_s K(x, sv_s) + b.
Calibrated posterior probabilities via Platt scaling. Requires probability=true at construction time. Returns a (n_samples, 2) matrix with columns [P(y=class_0), P(y=class_1)].

The figure below is rendered from a registered SkigenPlot-enabled example during the documentation build. Source example: examples/svm/svc.cpp constexpr int grid_side = 90; Eigen::MatrixXd grid(grid_side * grid_side, 2); const double x_min = X.col(0).minCoeff() - 0.6; const double x_max = X.col(0).maxCoeff() + 0.6; const double y_min = X.col(1).minCoeff() - 0.6; const double y_max = X.col(1).maxCoeff() + 0.6; int row = 0; for (int iy = 0; iy < grid_side; ++iy) { const double gy = y_min + (y_max - y_min) * iy / (grid_side - 1); for (int ix = 0; ix < grid_side; ++ix) { const double gx = x_min + (x_max - x_min) * ix / (grid_side - 1); grid(row, 0) = gx; grid(row, 1) = gy; ++row; } } const Eigen::VectorXi grid_labels = clf.predict(grid); Eigen::MatrixXd support_points(static_cast<Eigen::Index>(clf.support().size()), 2); Eigen::VectorXi support_labels(static_cast<Eigen::Index>(clf.support().size())); for (Eigen::Index i = 0; i < support_points.rows(); ++i) { const Eigen::Index sample = clf.support()[static_cast<std::size_t>(i)]; support_points.row(i) = X.row(sample); support_labels(i) = y(sample); } Skigen::Plot::Figure fig; fig.title("SVC Decision Regions") .caption("RBF-kernel Skigen::SVC decision regions with support vectors highlighted") .xlabel("feature 1") .ylabel("feature 2") .scatter(grid, grid_labels, {.pointSize = 2.0f, .opacity = 0.16f}) .scatter(X, y, {.pointSize = 7.0f, .opacity = 0.92f}) .scatter(support_points, support_labels, {.pointSize = 15.0f, .hollow = true}); return argc > 1 ? (fig.saveThemed(argv[1]) ? 0 : 1) : fig.show();

#include <Skigen/SVM> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::SVR(C=1.0, kernel=Kernel::RBF, degree=3, gamma=0, coef0=0, epsilon=0.1, tol=1e-3, max_iter=1000, random_state=std::nullopt) Epsilon-Support Vector Regression with kernels. Mirrors sklearn.svm.SVR. Solves the ϵ\epsilonϵ-insensitive primal in feature space using a sub-gradient SGD over the kernel-mapped dual coefficients. The solver is simpler than libsvm's full SMO-for-regression — it converges to the same minimiser at the optimum but the iterate trace differs. Attributes: C : Scalar epsilon : Scalar kernel : Kernel support : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & n_support : int
Fit from a sparse design matrix (densifies internally).

#include <Skigen/Tree> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=-1, min_samples_split=2, max_features_mode=0, max_features_value=0.0, random_state=std::nullopt) A decision tree classifier. A non-parametric supervised learning method used for classification. The model predicts the value of a target variable by learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features. Uses Gini impurity as the splitting criterion. Mirrors sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier. Attributes: classes : const Eigen::VectorXi n_classes : int feature_importances : RowVectorType
Build a decision tree classifier from the training set.

Fit on a sparse design matrix. The sparse input is converted to a dense MatrixType internally and forwarded to the regular split-finding path. The numerical results match a dense fit; a native sparse split finder (skip-zero column scans accumulating per-class counts only over explicit nonzeros) would avoid the densification but is not implemented.
Predict using a sparse design matrix (densifies internally).

Fit using a specific row index subset (used by RandomForest bootstraps). Parameters: sample_indices : const std::vector< Eigen::Index > & If empty, uses all rows of X. Otherwise, builds the tree from the rows specified (with possible repetitions).
Predict class labels for samples in X.
Per-class probability estimates from leaf class distributions.

#include <Skigen/Tree> template <typename Scalar = double> class Skigen::DecisionTreeRegressor(max_depth=-1, min_samples_split=2, max_features_mode=0, max_features_value=0.0, random_state=std::nullopt) A decision tree regressor. Uses MSE (Mean Squared Error) reduction as the splitting criterion. The prediction for a leaf node is the mean of the target values. Mirrors sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeRegressor. Attributes: feature_importances : RowVectorType n_targets : int
Build a decision tree regressor from the training set.

Fit on a sparse design matrix. The sparse input is converted to a dense MatrixType internally and forwarded to the regular split-finding path. The numerical results match a dense fit; native sparse split-finding is not implemented.
Predict using a sparse design matrix (densifies internally).
Return the R2R^2R2 coefficient of determination.

Fit on a multi-target response matrix Y (n_samples × n_targets). Implementation: fits one shared tree whose split criterion is the sum of target variances across all output columns, matching sklearn's joint multi-output tree structure. The single-target API (fit(X, y) / predict(X)) is unchanged; after fit_multi, the first target column's tree backs predict() for callers that expect a vector output.

Multi-target fit on a specific row index subset. Mirrors fit_with_indices for the single-target path but builds one shared joint tree with summed-variance split scoring. Used by ensemble bootstraps that need per-tree multi-output sample subsets.

Skigen follows the same directory convention as Eigen: Skigen/ ├── Core # Module header (no extension) ├── Preprocessing # Module header ├── Dense # Convenience header — bundles all modules └── src/ ├── Core/ │ ├── Traits.h # EigenTypes<Scalar> type aliases │ ├── Concepts.h # C++23 concepts (TransformerLike, PredictorLike) │ ├── Base.h # CRTP bases (Estimator, Transformer, Predictor) │ ├── Validation.h # Input validation │ └── EigenHelpers.h # Numerical utilities └── Preprocessing/ └── StandardScaler.h Users include Skigen exactly like Eigen: #include <Eigen/Dense> // Eigen #include <Skigen/Dense> // Skigen — same pattern #include <Skigen/Core> // or individual modules #include <Skigen/Preprocessing>
All estimators use the Curiously Recurring Template Pattern for zero-cost abstractions: No virtual functions. No vtable. The compiler resolves all dispatch at compile time.
All code lives under namespace Skigen. Internal helpers live under namespace Skigen::internal.
C++23 compiler (GCC ≥ 13, Clang ≥ 17, or MSVC ≥ 19.38) CMake ≥ 3.20 Eigen ≥ 3.4

git clone https://github.com/skigen-project/skigen.git cd skigen cmake -B build \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_TESTS=ON \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_EXAMPLES=ON \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_BENCHMARKS=ON cmake --build build If Eigen is not installed system-wide, place the Eigen source tree as a sibling directory: parent/ ├── eigen/ # Eigen source tree └── skigen/ # This repository CMake will detect it automatically.
Option Default Description SKIGEN_BUILD_TESTS ON Build the test suite SKIGEN_BUILD_BENCHMARKS OFF Build performance benchmarks SKIGEN_BUILD_EXAMPLES OFF Build example programs
Contributions are welcome. Please follow these guidelines.

C++23. Use modern features (concepts, [[nodiscard]], structured bindings) where they add clarity. No raw loops over matrix elements. Use Eigen expression templates. No virtual. Use CRTP for polymorphism. Warning-free under -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic.

Create the internal header at Skigen/src/Module/Name.h. Inherit from the appropriate CRTP base (Transformer or Predictor). Implement fit_impl, transform_impl / predict_impl, and accessors. Add the header to the module file (e.g. Skigen/Preprocessing). Write tests in tests/test_name.cpp. Add a parity script in tests/parity/. Document in doc/website/docs/guide/.

Every estimator must have: Unit tests for correctness. A Python parity script that generates reference data from scikit-learn. C++ parity tests that compare Skigen output against the reference. Tolerance: 1e-12 for double, 1e-5 for float.
Keep changes focused — one estimator or feature per PR. Ensure all tests pass before submitting. Include benchmark results if the change affects performance.
Skigen is built under the EES standard — a set of engineering constraints that ensure every cycle spent is a cycle of useful computation.

Machine learning code written in interpreted languages pays a constant overhead for object dispatch, garbage collection, and the GIL. Skigen eliminates this by compiling directly to vectorized machine code.

All estimators are templatized on Scalar (default: double). Switching to float doubles SIMD throughput on the same hardware: Skigen::StandardScaler<float> scaler; // 2× SIMD density Skigen::StandardScaler<double> scaler; // Full precision
All read-only inputs accept Eigen::Ref<const MatrixType>, which supports: Direct matrix references (no copy) Sub-block views Memory-mapped data
Eigen's expression templates fuse operations and vectorize automatically. Skigen never uses manual element-wise loops — all computation flows through Eigen's optimized kernel pipeline.

Every transformer provides _inplace variants that modify data directly, eliminating temporary allocations: scaler.transform_inplace(X); // No allocation Eigen::MatrixXd Z = scaler.transform(X); // Allocates result
CRTP replaces virtual dispatch. The compiler resolves all calls at compile time — no vtable, no indirect jumps, no branch mispredictions from polymorphism.

Throughput: Operations should achieve ≥ 80% of Eigen's raw kernel throughput. Memory: transform_inplace should use zero additional heap memory beyond the input. Float/Double ratio: float throughput should be ≈ 2× double throughput on AVX2 hardware.

Skigen v1.1.0 is the only release in the 1.x line that ships explicit breaking changes. The release theme (sklearn parity, sparse / streaming infrastructure, manifold + probabilistic depth) made a small number of API breaks unavoidable. From v1.2.0 onward, strict-additive semver resumes. This page enumerates every break and gives the smallest-diff upgrade for each one.

Why. Multi-target regression support (fit(X, Y) with a matrix Y) made a uniform 2-D representation mandatory. Affected estimators. LinearRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet, KNeighborsRegressor, DecisionTreeRegressor, BayesianRidge, ARDRegression. Before (v1.0.0): Skigen::Ridge<double> reg; reg.fit(X, y); Eigen::VectorXd w = reg.coef_(); // VectorType After (v1.1.0): Skigen::Ridge<double> reg; reg.fit(X, y); Eigen::MatrixXd W = reg.coef_(); // MatrixType (n_targets × n_features) Eigen::VectorXd w = reg.coef_().row(0).transpose(); // single-target slice // or, transitional helper: Eigen::VectorXd w_legacy = reg.coef_vec(); coef_vec() is a transitional convenience accessor available on every single-target regressor and will be removed in a future release.
documents.s: 1. Regressor coef_ is now a MatrixType

documents.t: Why. GridSearchCV and RandomizedSearchCV need a uniform reflection surface across all estimators. Affected. Every concrete estimator now inherits a set_param(name, value) and a get_params() method from the base. Default-only code paths are unchanged — you only see this if you use parameter search or pipelines with nested-parameter syntax. Authoring an estimator. Register parameters once with the SKIGEN_PARAMS macro: class MyEstimator : public Predictor<MyEstimator, double> { public: SKIGEN_PARAMS(("alpha", alpha_), ("max_iter", max_iter_), ("fit_intercept", fit_intercept_)); }; ParameterValue is std::variant<int, double, std::string, bool>. Existing estimators in Skigen have already been migrated.

Why. sklearn parity — both parameters existed in scikit-learn and were omitted by mistake in v1.0.0. Before: Skigen::LinearRegression<double> lr(/*fit_intercept=*/false); After: // Same constructor still works — fit_intercept retains its position. Skigen::LinearRegression<double> lr(/*fit_intercept=*/false); // New keyword-style constructor for the additional parameters: Skigen::LinearRegression<double> lr_pos(/*fit_intercept=*/true, /*positive=*/true, /*n_jobs=*/1); No call-site change is required unless you want the new behaviour.

Why. sklearn parity, and the "step__param" nested-parameter syntax required by GridSearchCV over pipelines. Before (positional, still supported): auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline(StandardScaler<double>{}, Ridge<double>{}); After (named, recommended when used with GridSearchCV): auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline( std::pair{"scaler", StandardScaler<double>{}}, std::pair{"ridge", Ridge<double>{}}); Skigen::ParameterGrid grid(Skigen::ParameterGrid::Grid{ {"ridge__alpha", {Skigen::ParameterValue(0.1), Skigen::ParameterValue(1.0)}}}); Skigen::GridSearchCV<decltype(pipe)> gs(pipe, grid, /*cv=*/5); Existing positional pipelines continue to compile and run unchanged.

Why. sklearn parity. f1_score(..., average=None) returns one score per class; average="macro" returns a scalar. The two cannot share a Scalar return type. Before: double f = Skigen::f1_score(y_true, y_pred); // average="binary" After: double f = Skigen::f1_score(y_true, y_pred); // unchanged Skigen::MetricsResult res = Skigen::f1_score( y_true, y_pred, Skigen::Average::None); // VectorType payload Eigen::VectorXd per_class = res.values(); MetricsResult exposes scalar(), values(), and is_scalar(). The single-scalar overload remains the default and is unaffected.

Why. sklearn parity — cluster_centers_ is (n_clusters, n_features). v1.0.0 already returned this shape but was not formally guaranteed; the v1.1.0 contract makes it mandatory and the parity tests enforce it. If your code read the centres as (n_features, n_clusters), transpose at the call site.

NuSVC, NuSVR, and OneClassSVM are exposed in the v1.1.0 public surface (constructors, parameter parity, and headers) but their fit method throws std::logic_error until the nu-SVM SMO variant lands. Use SVC (C-SVM) and SVR (epsilon-SVR) instead. The placeholder examples under examples/svm/ demonstrate the documented exception path.
documents.s: 7. nu-SVM family is documented as placeholder

documents.t: Audit every read of reg.coef_() — switch to coef_().row(0) or coef_vec() for single-target regressors. If you wrote a custom estimator, add SKIGEN_PARAMS(...). If you call f1_score / precision_score / recall_score with a non-scalar average, bind the result to MetricsResult. If you build a pipeline that will be passed to GridSearchCV, switch to the named-step form. Replace any direct use of NuSVC / NuSVR / OneClassSVM with the C-SVM / epsilon-SVR equivalents until the nu-SMO solver lands.
C++23 compiler (GCC ≥ 13, Clang ≥ 17, MSVC ≥ 19.38) Eigen ≥ 3.4 CMake ≥ 3.20 (for tests/examples)
Skigen is header-only. Clone it alongside Eigen and point your include path at the repository root: git clone https://github.com/skigen-project/skigen.git
find_package(Eigen3 3.4 REQUIRED) add_subdirectory(skigen) # or use find_package if installed target_link_libraries(my_app PRIVATE Skigen::skigen)
Add both the skigen repo root and your Eigen path to your compiler includes: g++ -std=c++23 -I/path/to/skigen -I/path/to/eigen my_app.cpp -o my_app

#include <Eigen/Dense> #include <Skigen/Dense> #include <iostream> int main() { // Create a dataset Eigen::MatrixXd X(5, 3); X << 1, -1, 2, 2, 0, 0, 0, 1, -1, 1, 1, 1, 3, -1, 0; // Standardize (zero mean, unit variance) Skigen::StandardScaler scaler; Eigen::MatrixXd Z = scaler.fit_transform(X); std::cout << "Standardized:
" << Z << "

"; std::cout << "Mean: " << scaler.mean() << "
"; std::cout << "Scale: " << scaler.scale() << "
"; // Round-trip back to original Eigen::MatrixXd X_back = scaler.inverse_transform(Z); std::cout << "
Recovered:
" << X_back << "
"; return 0; }

Agglomerative clustering builds a hierarchy bottom-up by repeatedly merging the closest active clusters. The fitted labels are produced by cutting the merge tree at n_clusters active clusters. Mirrors sklearn.cluster.AgglomerativeClustering for dense, brute-force linkage on Euclidean or Manhattan distances.

Skigen::AgglomerativeClustering<Scalar> model( int n_clusters = 2, std::string linkage = "ward", std::string metric = "euclidean"); Parameter Default Description n_clusters 2 Number of clusters at which to cut the tree. linkage "ward" Merge criterion: "ward", "single", "complete", or "average". metric "euclidean" Distance metric for non-Ward linkage: "euclidean", "l2", "manhattan", or "l1".
Method Description fit(X) Build the hierarchical merge tree. fit_predict(X) Fit and return labels for the training samples.
Accessor Description labels() Cluster label for each training sample. children() Merge tree children, shape (n_samples - 1, 2). distances() Distance or Ward cost stored for each merge.

//! [example_agglomerative_clustering] Skigen::AgglomerativeClustering<double> model( /*n_clusters=*/3, /*linkage=*/"ward", /*metric=*/"euclidean"); const Eigen::VectorXi labels = model.fit_predict(X); std::cout << "Agglomerative labels: " << labels.transpose() << "
"; std::cout << "Merge tree shape: " << model.children().rows() << " x " << model.children().cols() << "
"; std::cout << "First merge: " << model.children().row(0) << "
"; //! [example_agglomerative_clustering]

This first implementation targets medium-size dense datasets with deterministic brute-force pair selection. It does not yet implement connectivity constraints, distance thresholds, sparse input, precomputed distances, or full sklearn tree-building options.
Anomaly estimators assign higher scores to inliers and lower scores to outliers. Skigen v1.2.0 includes dense numeric implementations of EllipticEnvelope and IsolationForest.

EllipticEnvelope fits an empirical Gaussian covariance model and scores samples by negative Mahalanobis distance. Skigen::EllipticEnvelope<double> model( contamination = 0.1, assume_centered = false, regularization = 1e-9); model.fit(X); Eigen::VectorXi labels = model.predict(X_test);

IsolationForest builds random isolation trees. Samples isolated by shorter average paths receive lower score_samples values. Skigen::IsolationForest<double> model( n_estimators = 100, max_samples = 256, contamination = 0.1, max_depth = -1, random_state = 7); model.fit(X); Eigen::VectorXd scores = model.score_samples(X_test);

Method Description fit(X) Fit the detector on dense numeric samples. score_samples(X) Return higher-is-more-normal anomaly scores. decision_function(X) Return scores shifted by offset(). predict(X) Return 1 for inliers and -1 for outliers.

Accessor Description offset() Score threshold selected from contamination. location() EllipticEnvelope fitted center. covariance() EllipticEnvelope covariance estimate. precision() EllipticEnvelope precision matrix. dist() EllipticEnvelope fitted Mahalanobis distances. max_samples_effective() IsolationForest resolved sample count per tree.

Mirrors the dense scoring and labeling semantics of sklearn.covariance.EllipticEnvelope and sklearn.ensemble.IsolationForest. Robust minimum-covariance determinant support, sparse input, warm starts, bootstrap sampling, feature subsampling, parallel fitting, and contamination="auto" are deferred. API Reference For full signatures see the EllipticEnvelope API Reference and IsolationForest API Reference.
Automatic Relevance Determination: Bayesian linear regression with a separate weight precision per feature, driving irrelevant coefficients to zero for sparse solutions.
Identical evidence-maximisation machinery to BayesianRidge but with a per-feature lambda. Features whose precision exceeds threshold_lambda are pruned from the model.
Skigen::ARDRegression<Scalar> model(int max_iter = 300, Scalar tol = 1e-3, ..., Scalar threshold_lambda = 1e4);
Parameter Default Description max_iter 300 Iterations. tol 1e-3 Convergence tolerance. threshold_lambda 1e4 Prune features above this precision. compute_score false Track the marginal likelihood.
Method Description fit(X, y) Optimise the per-feature evidence. predict(X) Predictive mean. predict(X, with_std) Mean and predictive std-dev.
Accessor Description coef() Posterior mean weights (sparse). alpha() Noise precision. lambda_() Per-feature weight precisions.

Skigen::ARDRegression<double> ard; ard.fit(X, y); auto preds = ard.predict(X_test); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. See the generator tests/parity/generate_bayesian_linear_reference.py and the reference fixtures in tests/parity/data/ard_regression/, exercised by tests/parity/parity_bayesian_linear.cpp. API Reference For full signatures see the ARDRegression API Reference.
Bayesian ridge regression: a Gaussian linear model whose noise and weight precisions are estimated from the data by evidence maximisation, yielding predictive uncertainty for free.

Gamma hyperpriors on the noise precision (alpha) and weight precision (lambda) are optimised by the iterative evidence-maximisation scheme of Tipping (2001). predict(X, with_std) returns the predictive mean and standard deviation.
Skigen::BayesianRidge<Scalar> model(int max_iter = 300, Scalar tol = 1e-3, Scalar alpha_1 = 1e-6, ...);

Parameter Default Description max_iter 300 Evidence-maximisation iterations. tol 1e-3 Convergence tolerance. alpha_1 / alpha_2 1e-6 Gamma prior on the noise precision. lambda_1 / lambda_2 1e-6 Gamma prior on the weight precision. compute_score false Track the log marginal likelihood.
Method Description fit(X, y) Optimise the evidence. predict(X) Predictive mean. predict(X, with_std) Mean and predictive std-dev.
Accessor Description coef() Posterior mean weights. alpha() Estimated noise precision. lambda_() Estimated weight precision.

Skigen::BayesianRidge<double> br; br.fit(X, y); auto [mean, std] = br.predict(X_test, Skigen::with_std); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. See the generator tests/parity/generate_bayesian_linear_reference.py and the reference fixtures in tests/parity/data/bayesian_ridge/, exercised by tests/parity/parity_bayesian_linear.cpp. API Reference For full signatures see the BayesianRidge API Reference.
Bernoulli Naive Bayes for binary/boolean features, with an explicit penalty for features that do not occur. Sparse-aware.

Inputs are binarised at binarize. Each class stores the smoothed probability that each feature is 1; the decision function includes the log-probability of absence for unset features, distinguishing it from the multinomial model.
Skigen::BernoulliNB<Scalar> model(Scalar alpha = 1.0, bool fit_prior = true, std::optional<Scalar> binarize = 0.0);
Parameter Default Description alpha 1.0 Additive smoothing. fit_prior true Learn class priors. binarize 0.0 Threshold for binarising features; nullopt assumes pre-binarised input.
Method Description fit(X, y) Accumulate binary feature counts. partial_fit(X, y, classes) Incremental update. predict(X) MAP class labels. predict_proba(X) Class posteriors.
Accessor Description feature_log_prob() Log P(feature = 1 class_log_prior() Log class priors.

Skigen::BernoulliNB<double> nb; nb.fit(X_binary, y); auto preds = nb.predict(X_test); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. See the generator tests/parity/generate_naive_bayes_reference.py and the reference fixtures in tests/parity/data/bernoulli_nb/, exercised by tests/parity/parity_naive_bayes.cpp. API Reference For full signatures see the BernoulliNB API Reference.

BIRCH compresses samples into clustering-feature subclusters before a final global clustering step. This is useful for larger dense datasets where repeatedly revisiting every sample is more expensive than clustering a smaller summary. Mirrors the dense core of sklearn.cluster.Birch.

Skigen::Birch<Scalar> model(Scalar threshold = 0.5, int n_clusters = 3); Parameter Default Description threshold 0.5 Maximum radius allowed when absorbing a sample into an existing CF subcluster. n_clusters 3 Number of final global clusters.

Method Description fit(X) Compress samples into CF subclusters and fit final centers. fit_predict(X) Fit and return labels for the training samples. predict(X) Assign new samples to fitted final centers.
Accessor Description cluster_centers() Final cluster centers. subcluster_centers() CF subcluster centers from the compression pass. labels() Cluster label for each training sample.

//! [example_birch] Skigen::Birch<double> birch(/*threshold=*/0.45, /*n_clusters=*/3); const Eigen::VectorXi labels = birch.fit_predict(X); std::cout << "Birch labels: " << labels.transpose() << "
"; std::cout << "Subcluster centers: " << birch.subcluster_centers().rows() << "
"; std::cout << "Cluster centers:
" << birch.cluster_centers() << "
"; //! [example_birch]

This first version uses a flat CF-subcluster pass and a deterministic KMeans-style final clustering step. It does not yet implement sklearn's full branching CF tree, branching_factor, partial_fit, compute_labels=false, sparse input, or a custom final clustering estimator.

Post-hoc probability calibration that maps a base classifier's scores onto well-calibrated probabilities using sigmoid (Platt) or isotonic regression fit by cross-validation. The examples/calibration/calibrated_classifier_cv.cpp program renders a reliability curve comparing predicted positive-class probabilities with observed positive fractions:

For each CV fold the base estimator is fit on the training part and a calibrator (1-D logistic regression for sigmoid, PAVA for isotonic) is fit on the held-out scores. Predictions average the per-fold calibrators. v1.1.0 covers binary classification.
Skigen::CalibratedClassifierCV<Base, Scalar> model(Base est, Method = Sigmoid, int cv = 5, int n_jobs = 1, bool ensemble = true);
Parameter Default Description estimator — Base classifier to calibrate. method Sigmoid Sigmoid (Platt) or Isotonic. cv 5 Cross-validation folds. ensemble true Average per-fold calibrators.
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit base estimators and calibrators. predict(X) Calibrated class labels. predict_proba(X) Calibrated probabilities.
Accessor Description n_classes() Number of classes. n_estimators_fitted() Calibrated base estimators held.

Skigen::GaussianNB<double> nb; Skigen::CalibratedClassifierCV cc(nb, Skigen::CalibrationMethod::Sigmoid, 5); cc.fit(X, y); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. See the generator tests/parity/generate_calibration_reference.py and the reference fixtures in tests/parity/data/calibrated_classifier_cv/, exercised by tests/parity/parity_calibration.cpp. API Reference For full signatures see the CalibratedClassifierCV API Reference.

Cross-decomposition estimators learn paired latent spaces from dense X and Y matrices. Skigen v1.2.0 includes: PLSRegression, a NIPALS partial least-squares regressor for dense multi-output targets. CCA, a NIPALS canonical-correlation estimator for paired dense matrices.

PLSRegression extracts latent components from X that explain covariance with Y, then fits a linear prediction map back to the original target scale. Skigen::PLSRegression<double> pls( n_components = 2, scale = true, max_iter = 500, tol = 1e-6); pls.fit(X, Y); Eigen::MatrixXd prediction = pls.predict(X_test);

CCA learns paired rotations for X and Y whose transformed scores have high linear correlation. Skigen::CCA<double> cca( n_components = 2, scale = true, max_iter = 500, tol = 1e-6); cca.fit(X, Y); auto [x_scores, y_scores] = cca.transform(X, Y);

Method Description fit(X, Y) Fit paired latent components with NIPALS. transform(X) Project X into latent component space. transform(X, Y) Project both views and return paired score matrices. predict(X) Predict Y from X using the fitted linear map. score(X, Y) Return multi-output R2R^2R2 for predict(X).

Accessor Description x_weights() / y_weights() NIPALS weight vectors. x_loadings() / y_loadings() Deflation loadings. x_scores() / y_scores() Training latent scores. x_rotations() / y_rotations() Projection rotations used by transform. coef() Original-scale linear prediction coefficients. intercept() Original-scale prediction intercept. n_iter() Iterations used per component.

Mirrors the dense NIPALS core of sklearn.cross_decomposition.PLSRegression and sklearn.cross_decomposition.CCA. The generated API entries link to the sklearn reference pages. Sparse input, sample weights, feature-name propagation, copy, and the broader PLSCanonical / PLSSVD family are deferred. API Reference For full signatures see the PLSRegression API Reference and CCA API Reference.

DBSCAN finds density-connected regions without requiring the number of clusters in advance. Samples that are not reachable from a core sample are labelled -1 as noise. Mirrors sklearn.cluster.DBSCAN for dense, brute-force Euclidean and Manhattan distances.

Skigen::DBSCAN<Scalar> model(Scalar eps = 0.5, int min_samples = 5, std::string metric = "euclidean"); Parameter Default Description eps 0.5 Maximum radius for neighbourhood membership. min_samples 5 Minimum number of samples in an eps-neighbourhood for a core sample, including the sample itself. metric "euclidean" Distance metric: "euclidean", "l2", "manhattan", or "l1".
Method Description fit(X) Fit density-connected clusters. fit_predict(X) Fit and return labels for the training samples.
Accessor Description labels() Cluster label for each training sample; -1 marks noise. core_sample_indices() Indices of core samples. components() Dense rows corresponding to core samples.

//! [example_dbscan] Skigen::DBSCAN<double> dbscan(/*eps=*/0.45, /*min_samples=*/4); const Eigen::VectorXi labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X); std::cout << "DBSCAN labels: " << labels.transpose() << "
"; std::cout << "Core samples: " << dbscan.core_sample_indices().size() << "
"; std::cout << "Components: " << dbscan.components().rows() << " x " << dbscan.components().cols() << "
"; //! [example_dbscan]
The implementation is intentionally brute-force and deterministic. It does not yet expose sklearn's algorithm, leaf_size, p, sample_weight, sparse precomputed distances, or parallel execution knobs.

A CART (Classification and Regression Trees) decision tree classifier. The algorithm recursively partitions the feature space with binary splits, choosing at each node the feature and threshold that best separate the classes.

Gini impurity measures the probability that a randomly chosen sample from node ttt would be misclassified if labeled according to the class distribution at that node: Gini(t)=1−∑k=1Kpk2\text{Gini}(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2Gini(t)=1−k=1∑K​pk2​ where pkp_kpk​ is the proportion of class-kkk samples at node ttt. A pure node (Gini=0\text{Gini} = 0Gini=0) contains only one class. At each split, the algorithm searches over all features and thresholds for the partition that minimizes the weighted Gini impurity of the two child nodes: Ginisplit=nLn Gini(tL)+nRn Gini(tR)\text{Gini}_{\text{split}} = \frac{n_L}{n} \,\text{Gini}(t_L) + \frac{n_R}{n} \,\text{Gini}(t_R)Ginisplit​=nnL​​Gini(tL​)+nnR​​Gini(tR​) The information gain of a split is Gini(t)−Ginisplit\text{Gini}(t) - \text{Gini}_{\text{split}}Gini(t)−Ginisplit​.

The tree is built recursively using the CART algorithm: At each node, evaluate every possible split (j,θ)(j, \theta)(j,θ) — feature jjj, threshold θ\thetaθ — and select the one that minimizes Ginisplit\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}Ginisplit​. Create left child (Xj≤θX_j \le \thetaXj​≤θ) and right child (Xj>θX_j > \thetaXj​>θ). Recurse until a stopping condition is met: max_depth reached, fewer than min_samples_split samples, or the node is pure. Prediction assigns the majority class of the leaf node reached by the query sample.

Interpretability: Decision trees are easy to visualize and explain. No scaling needed: Trees are invariant to monotonic transformations of features. High variance: Single trees are prone to overfitting — consider ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) for better generalization. Mirrors sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier.

Skigen::DecisionTreeClassifier<Scalar> tree(int max_depth = -1, int min_samples_split = 2); Parameter Default Description max_depth -1 Maximum tree depth (−1-1−1 = unlimited) min_samples_split 2 Minimum samples required to split a node
Method Description fit(X, y) Build the decision tree predict(X) Predict class labels score(X, y) Return classification accuracy
#include <Skigen/Tree> Skigen::DecisionTreeClassifier tree(/*max_depth=*/5); tree.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "Accuracy: " << tree.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n";

Discriminant analysis classifiers model class-conditional Gaussian distributions and use Bayes' rule for classification. Skigen v1.2.0 includes dense numeric implementations of: LinearDiscriminantAnalysis, which shares one covariance matrix across classes and produces linear decision boundaries. QuadraticDiscriminantAnalysis, which fits one covariance matrix per class and produces quadratic decision boundaries.

LDA fits class means, inferred priors, and a pooled covariance matrix. The class score is: xTΣ−1μk−12μkTΣ−1μk+log⁡πkx^T\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k - \frac{1}{2}\mu_k^T\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k + \log \pi_kxTΣ−1μk​−21​μkT​Σ−1μk​+logπk​ The predicted class is the one with the highest score.

QDA fits one covariance matrix per class. The class score is: −12((x−μk)TΣk−1(x−μk)+log⁡∣Σk∣)+log⁡πk-\frac{1}{2}\left((x-\mu_k)^T\Sigma_k^{-1}(x-\mu_k) + \log |\Sigma_k|\right) + \log \pi_k−21​((x−μk​)TΣk−1​(x−μk​)+log∣Σk​∣)+logπk​ reg_param blends each class covariance with the identity matrix, which can stabilize small or nearly rank-deficient datasets.

Method Description fit(X, y) Fit class priors, means, and covariance model. predict(X) Predict integer class labels. decision_function(X) Return class scores. predict_proba(X) Return normalized class probabilities. predict_log_proba(X) Return log probabilities.

Accessor Description classes() Sorted class labels. priors() Inferred class priors. means() Class means. covariance() Pooled covariance for LDA, per-class covariances for QDA. coef() LDA class coefficients. intercept() LDA class intercepts.

#include <Skigen/DiscriminantAnalysis> Skigen::LinearDiscriminantAnalysis<double> lda; lda.fit(X, y); Eigen::VectorXi labels = lda.predict(X_test); Skigen::QuadraticDiscriminantAnalysis<double> qda(1e-6); qda.fit(X, y); Eigen::MatrixXd probabilities = qda.predict_proba(X_test);

Mirrors the dense classification core of sklearn.discriminant_analysis.LinearDiscriminantAnalysis and sklearn.discriminant_analysis.QuadraticDiscriminantAnalysis. Solver variants, shrinkage modes, custom priors, covariance estimators, sparse input, sample weights, feature names, and LDA dimensionality reduction are deferred. API Reference For full signatures see the LinearDiscriminantAnalysis API Reference and QuadraticDiscriminantAnalysis API Reference.

ElasticNet combines ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ (Lasso) and ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ (Ridge) regularization, inheriting sparsity from Lasso and stability from Ridge. It is particularly useful when features are correlated: Lasso tends to arbitrarily select one feature from a correlated group, while ElasticNet distributes weight across them.

min⁡w12n∥Xw−y∥22+α(ρ∥w∥1+1−ρ2∥w∥22)\min_w \frac{1}{2n} \|Xw - y\|_2^2 + \alpha \left( \rho \|w\|_1 + \frac{1-\rho}{2} \|w\|_2^2 \right)wmin​2n1​∥Xw−y∥22​+α(ρ∥w∥1​+21−ρ​∥w∥22​) where ρ∈[0,1]\rho \in [0, 1]ρ∈[0,1] is the l1_ratio controlling the mix between ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ and ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ penalties: ρ=1\rho = 1ρ=1: pure Lasso ρ=0\rho = 0ρ=0: pure Ridge (scaled by 1/(2n)1/(2n)1/(2n)) This formulation matches scikit-learn's ElasticNet.

Like Lasso, ElasticNet is solved via coordinate descent. The update for coefficient jjj is: wj←S(ρj, nαρ)∥Xj∥22+nα(1−ρ)w_j \leftarrow \frac{S(\rho_j,\; n \alpha \rho)}{\|X_j\|_2^2 + n \alpha (1 - \rho)}wj​←∥Xj​∥22​+nα(1−ρ)S(ρj​,nαρ)​ where ρj=Xj⊤(y−X∖j w∖j)\rho_j = X_j^\top(y - X_{\setminus j}\, w_{\setminus j})ρj​=Xj⊤​(y−X∖j​w∖j​) and SSS is the soft-thresholding operator. The ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ term adds nα(1−ρ)n\alpha(1-\rho)nα(1−ρ) to the denominator, preventing the instability that Lasso can exhibit with correlated features.

Correlated features: ElasticNet is preferred over Lasso when features are highly correlated. Grouped selection: The ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ component encourages correlated features to be selected together. Regularization path: ElasticNet's convex combination provides a smooth transition between Ridge and Lasso behavior.

Skigen::ElasticNet<Scalar> model(Scalar alpha = 1, Scalar l1_ratio = 0.5, bool fit_intercept = true, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-4); Parameter Default Description alpha 1 Overall regularization strength (α≥0\alpha \ge 0α≥0) l1_ratio 0.5 Mix ratio ρ\rhoρ (111 = Lasso, 000 = Ridge) fit_intercept true Whether to center the data and compute an intercept max_iter 1000 Maximum coordinate descent iterations tol 1e-4 Convergence tolerance
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the model via coordinate descent predict(X) Predict y^=Xw+b\hat{y} = Xw + by^​=Xw+b score(X, y) Return the R2R^2R2 coefficient of determination
Accessor Type Description coef() RowVectorType Estimated coefficients intercept() Scalar Intercept term

Centres the data (unless assume_centered) and forms the 1/n scatter matrix. The maximum-likelihood normalisation matches scikit-learn.

A latent linear-Gaussian model that explains observed features as a few common factors plus per-feature (heteroscedastic) noise — unlike PCA, which assumes isotropic noise.
Loadings and noise variances are fit by expectation-maximisation. Loadings are identifiable only up to rotation, so downstream use should rely on the implied covariance or the transformed factors.
Skigen::FactorAnalysis<Scalar> model(int n_components = 0, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-3);
Parameter Default Description n_components 0 Number of latent factors. max_iter 1000 EM iterations. tol 1e-3 Log-likelihood convergence tolerance.
Method Description fit(X) Run EM. transform(X) Project onto the latent factors. get_covariance() Model-implied covariance.
Accessor Description components() Loading matrix W. noise_variance() Per-feature noise variance. log_likelihood() Final log-likelihood.

Skigen::FactorAnalysis<double> fa(2); fa.fit(X); auto Z = fa.transform(X); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. Gaussian Naive Bayes: each feature is modelled as class-conditional Gaussian, and predictions combine the per-feature likelihoods with the class priors under the naive independence assumption.

For each class the per-feature mean and variance are estimated by maximum likelihood (a small var_smoothing floor is added for stability). Posterior class scores are the sum of Gaussian log-likelihoods plus the log prior. Supports streaming via partial_fit with Welford-style variance updates.
Parameter Default Description priors empty Class priors; estimated from data if empty. var_smoothing 1e-9 Variance floor as a fraction of the largest feature variance.
Method Description fit(X, y) Estimate per-class means and variances. partial_fit(X, y, classes) Incremental update. predict(X) MAP class labels. predict_proba(X) Normalised class posteriors.
Accessor Description theta() Per-class feature means. var() Per-class feature variances. class_prior() Class prior probabilities.

Skigen::GaussianNB<double> nb; nb.fit(X, y); auto proba = nb.predict_proba(X_test); Verified against scikit-learn This estimator is checked by the parity suite. See the generator tests/parity/generate_naive_bayes_reference.py and the reference fixtures in tests/parity/data/gaussian_nb/, exercised by tests/parity/parity_naive_bayes.cpp. API Reference For full signatures see the GaussianNB API Reference.
GaussianProcessClassifier fits a dense binary Gaussian Process classifier with a logistic likelihood and Laplace posterior approximation.

The classifier learns latent function values f under a Gaussian-process prior and a Bernoulli likelihood: p(yi=1∣fi)=σ(fi)p(y_i = 1 \mid f_i) = \sigma(f_i)p(yi​=1∣fi​)=σ(fi​) Skigen uses Newton-Laplace iterations to approximate the posterior over f, then predicts class probabilities from the posterior latent mean and variance.

using GPC = Skigen::GaussianProcessClassifier<double>; GPC model( kernel = GPC::Kernel::RBF, alpha = 1e-10, length_scale = 1.0, constant_value = 1.0, noise_level = 1.0, nu = 1.5, rational_quadratic_alpha = 1.0, periodicity = 1.0, sigma_0 = 1.0, max_iter = 100, tol = 1e-6);

Method Description fit(X, y) Fit a binary dense Gaussian Process classifier. predict(X) Return class labels. predict_proba(X) Return two class-probability columns ordered by classes(). decision_function(X) Return posterior latent mean scores. score(X, y) Return mean accuracy.

Accessor Description classes() Sorted binary class labels. X_train() Training samples retained for kernel prediction. latent_mean() Fitted latent function values at training samples. dual_coef() Posterior dual coefficients. n_iter() Number of Laplace iterations used.

Mirrors the dense fixed-hyperparameter binary subset of sklearn.gaussian_process.GaussianProcessClassifier. Multiclass one-vs-rest/one-vs-one modes, hyperparameter optimization, callable/composite kernels, sample weights, sparse input, and kernel sums/products are deferred. API Reference For full signatures see the GaussianProcessClassifier API Reference.
GaussianProcessRegressor fits a Bayesian non-parametric regression model using a dense covariance matrix and a Cholesky solve. It provides both posterior means and uncertainty estimates.

For training samples X and targets y, Skigen solves: (K(X,X)+αI)a=y(K(X, X) + \alpha I)a = y(K(X,X)+αI)a=y The posterior mean at test samples is: y^(X∗)=K(X∗,X)a\hat{y}(X_*) = K(X_*, X)ay^​(X∗​)=K(X∗​,X)a Posterior covariance is computed with the Cholesky factor of the regularized Gram matrix, matching the standard Gaussian-process conditioning equations.

The first Skigen implementation supports fixed-hyperparameter dense kernels: Kernel Notes RBF Isotropic squared-exponential kernel with length_scale. Matern Supports nu = 0.5, 1.5, and 2.5. RationalQuadratic Uses rational_quadratic_alpha. ExpSineSquared Periodic kernel with periodicity. DotProduct Linear dot-product kernel with sigma_0. White Diagonal noise kernel with noise_level. Constant Constant covariance value. For stationary kernels, constant_value acts as the kernel amplitude.

using GPR = Skigen::GaussianProcessRegressor<double>; GPR model( kernel = GPR::Kernel::RBF, alpha = 1e-10, length_scale = 1.0, constant_value = 1.0, noise_level = 1.0, nu = 1.5, rational_quadratic_alpha = 1.0, periodicity = 1.0, sigma_0 = 1.0, normalize_y = false);

Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the dense Cholesky system. predict(X) Return posterior mean predictions. predict_std(X) Return posterior standard deviations. predict_covariance(X) Return posterior covariance matrix. score(X, y) Return R2R^2R2.

Accessor Description X_train() Training samples retained for kernel prediction. dual_coef() Cholesky-solved dual coefficients. L() Lower Cholesky factor of K + alpha I. y_train_mean() Mean subtracted when normalize_y=true. log_marginal_likelihood_value() Log marginal likelihood for the fixed kernel hyperparameters.

#include <Skigen/GaussianProcess> using GPR = Skigen::GaussianProcessRegressor<double>; GPR model(GPR::Kernel::RBF, 1e-6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.0, 1.5, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, true); model.fit(X, y); Eigen::VectorXd mean = model.predict(X_test); Eigen::VectorXd std = model.predict_std(X_test);

Mirrors the dense fixed-hyperparameter subset of sklearn.gaussian_process.GaussianProcessRegressor. Hyperparameter optimization, callable/composite kernels, kernel sums/products, multi-output targets, sample weights, and sparse input are deferred for the regressor. Binary classification is available through GaussianProcessClassifier. API Reference For full signatures see the GaussianProcess API Reference.
A stage-wise additive model that fits shallow regression trees to the negative gradient of the binary log-loss.

Starting from the log-odds prior, each stage fits a regression tree to the pseudo-residuals (negative gradient) of the loss and adds it to the running prediction scaled by learning_rate. Binary log-loss is supported; multiclass and the exponential loss are rejected with a parity-gap message.
Skigen::GradientBoostingClassifier<Scalar> model(Loss = LogLoss, Scalar learning_rate = 0.1, int n_estimators = 100, ...);

Exhaustive cross-validated search over a parameter grid, refitting the best configuration on the full training set.

Every point in the Cartesian product of the grid is evaluated by K-fold cross-validation. Grid points are dispatched across threads with n_jobs (parallelism is over the grid, not the folds). Over a pipeline, parameters are addressed by step index, e.g. 1__alpha.

Histogram-based gradient boosting: features are binned up-front so split finding scans bin histograms rather than raw values, making training near-linear in the sample count.

Each feature is quantile-binned into at most max_bins buckets. Split finding then operates on per-bin gradient/hessian histograms, which is dramatically faster on large datasets. Binary log-loss is supported.

Missing-value imputation fills incomplete numeric matrices before they enter estimators that require finite values. Skigen v1.2.0 introduces the dense numeric subset of scikit-learn's imputation API: SimpleImputer replaces missing values feature-wise with the mean, median, most frequent value, or a constant. MissingIndicator returns a 0/1 mask showing where missing values appear.

SimpleImputer computes one statistic per input feature during fit(X) and uses those statistics during transform(X). Strategy Behavior Mean Replace missing values with the non-missing feature mean. Median Replace missing values with the non-missing feature median. MostFrequent Replace missing values with the most common value; ties choose the smallest value. Constant Replace missing values with fill_value. All-missing features are dropped by default. With keep_empty_features=true, they are retained and filled with 0, or with fill_value for Constant.
MissingIndicator emits numeric masks with 0 for observed values and 1 for missing values. MissingOnly tracks only features that were missing during fit; All tracks every feature.

Skigen::SimpleImputer<double> imputer( missing_values = NaN, strategy = Skigen::ImputeStrategy::Mean, fill_value = 0.0, add_indicator = false, keep_empty_features = false); Skigen::MissingIndicator<double> indicator( missing_values = NaN, features = Skigen::MissingIndicatorFeatures::MissingOnly, error_on_new = true);

K-Means partitions nnn samples into KKK clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (inertia). The examples/cluster/kmeans.cpp program groups three Gaussian clusters and renders them with SkigenPlot:

min⁡{Ck,μk}∑k=1K∑xi∈Ck∥xi−μk∥22\min_{\{C_k, \mu_k\}} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|_2^2{Ck​,μk​}min​k=1∑K​xi​∈Ck​∑​∥xi​−μk​∥22​ where μk=1∣Ck∣∑xi∈Ckxi\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_iμk​=∣Ck​∣1​∑xi​∈Ck​​xi​ is the centroid of cluster CkC_kCk​.

K-Means uses Lloyd's algorithm, which alternates two steps until convergence: Assignment: Assign each sample to the cluster with the nearest centroid: Ck={xi:∥xi−μk∥≤∥xi−μj∥ ∀ j}C_k = \{x_i : \|x_i - \mu_k\| \le \|x_i - \mu_j\| \;\forall\, j\}Ck​={xi​:∥xi​−μk​∥≤∥xi​−μj​∥∀j} Update: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned samples: μk←1∣Ck∣∑xi∈Ckxi\mu_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_iμk​←∣Ck​∣1​∑xi​∈Ck​​xi​ The algorithm terminates when assignments no longer change or max_iter is reached. The result is a local minimum — n_init independent runs with different initializations are performed, and the solution with the lowest inertia is kept.

To avoid poor local minima, Skigen uses the k-means++ initialization strategy: Choose the first centroid μ1\mu_1μ1​ uniformly at random from the data. For each subsequent centroid μk\mu_kμk​, sample a data point xix_ixi​ with probability proportional to D(xi)2D(x_i)^2D(xi​)2, where D(xi)D(x_i)D(xi​) is the distance from xix_ixi​ to its nearest existing centroid. This initialization spreads the initial centroids apart, leading to faster convergence and better final solutions (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007).
Each iteration of Lloyd's algorithm costs O(nKp)O(nKp)O(nKp), where nnn is the number of samples, KKK is the number of clusters, and ppp is the number of features. Mirrors sklearn.cluster.KMeans.

Instance-based learning methods that make predictions by finding the kkk closest training samples in feature space. No explicit model is trained — the training data itself serves as the model.
Distances are computed using the Euclidean (ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​) metric: d(x,x′)=∑j=1p(xj−xj′)2d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} (x_j - x'_j)^2}d(x,x′)=j=1∑p​(xj​−xj′​)2​

Given a query point xxx, the classifier identifies the kkk nearest neighbors Nk(x)\mathcal{N}_k(x)Nk​(x) and predicts the most frequent class label among them: y^=arg⁡max⁡c∑xi∈Nk(x)1(yi=c)\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(x)} \mathbf{1}(y_i = c)y^​=argcmax​xi​∈Nk​(x)∑​1(yi​=c) In case of a tie, the class with the smallest index is selected.
The regressor predicts the mean target value of the kkk nearest neighbors: y^=1k∑xi∈Nk(x)yi\hat{y} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(x)} y_iy^​=k1​xi​∈Nk​(x)∑​yi​

Skigen uses brute-force search: prediction costs O(np)O(np)O(np) per query point, where nnn is the number of training samples and ppp is the number of features. This is practical for small to moderate datasets.

Non-linear boundaries: KNN naturally captures complex decision boundaries without assuming a parametric form. Small datasets: Works well when the dataset fits in memory and nnn is not too large. Feature scaling: Euclidean distance is sensitive to feature scales — standardize features before use (e.g., with StandardScaler). The choice of kkk trades off bias and variance: small kkk is flexible but noisy; large kkk is smoother but may over-smooth. Mirrors sklearn.neighbors.KNeighborsClassifier and KNeighborsRegressor.

The Lasso (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) is a linear regression model with an ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ penalty that induces sparsity — it drives some coefficients exactly to zero, effectively performing feature selection.

min⁡w12n∥Xw−y∥22+α∥w∥1\min_w \frac{1}{2n} \|Xw - y\|_2^2 + \alpha \|w\|_1wmin​2n1​∥Xw−y∥22​+α∥w∥1​ where nnn is the number of samples and α≥0\alpha \ge 0α≥0 controls the regularization strength. This formulation matches scikit-learn's Lasso.

Skigen solves the Lasso via coordinate descent with soft-thresholding. At each iteration, the algorithm optimizes one coefficient wjw_jwj​ at a time while holding all others fixed. Define the partial residual correlation: ρj=Xj⊤ ⁣(y−X∖j w∖j)\rho_j = X_j^\top\!\left(y - X_{\setminus j}\, w_{\setminus j}\right)ρj​=Xj⊤​(y−X∖j​w∖j​) where X∖jX_{\setminus j}X∖j​ denotes all columns except jjj. The closed-form update for coordinate jjj is: wj←S(ρj, nα)∥Xj∥22w_j \leftarrow \frac{S(\rho_j,\; n\alpha)}{\|X_j\|_2^2}wj​←∥Xj​∥22​S(ρj​,nα)​ where S(z,γ)=sign⁡(z)max⁡(∣z∣−γ, 0)S(z, \gamma) = \operatorname{sign}(z)\max(|z| - \gamma,\, 0)S(z,γ)=sign(z)max(∣z∣−γ,0) is the soft-thresholding operator. In practice, ρj\rho_jρj​ is computed efficiently using the running residual r=y−Xwr = y - Xwr=y−Xw: ρj=Xj⊤r+∥Xj∥22 wjold\rho_j = X_j^\top r + \|X_j\|_2^2 \, w_j^{\text{old}}ρj​=Xj⊤​r+∥Xj​∥22​wjold​ The algorithm cycles over all features until convergence (maximum coefficient change <tol< \texttt{tol}<tol) or max_iter iterations.

The ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ penalty produces sparse solutions: at convergence, any coefficient whose partial correlation ∣ρj∣|\rho_j|∣ρj​∣ does not exceed nαn\alphanα is set exactly to zero. This makes Lasso a natural tool for feature selection in high-dimensional settings.

Feature selection: When you expect many features to be irrelevant. Sparse signals: When the true underlying model involves only a few non-zero coefficients. For correlated features, consider ElasticNet, which combines ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ and ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ penalties for stability.

Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) linear regression. Fits a linear model y^=Xw+b\hat{y} = Xw + by^​=Xw+b by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.

w^=arg⁡min⁡w∥Xw−y∥22\hat{w} = \arg\min_w \|Xw - y\|_2^2w^=argwmin​∥Xw−y∥22​ The gradient condition X⊤(Xw−y)=0X^\top(Xw - y) = 0X⊤(Xw−y)=0 yields the normal equation: X⊤X w=X⊤yX^\top X\, w = X^\top yX⊤Xw=X⊤y

Skigen solves the least squares problem via Eigen::ColPivHouseholderQR, which computes a column-pivoted QR decomposition of XXX. This is numerically more stable than forming and inverting X⊤XX^\top XX⊤X directly, and it correctly handles rank-deficient design matrices. The factorization X=QRP⊤X = QRP^\topX=QRP⊤ yields the solution w=R−1Q⊤yw = R^{-1}Q^\top yw=R−1Q⊤y (with appropriate permutation), and also provides the numerical rank of XXX.
The QR factorization costs O(np2)O(np^2)O(np2) where nnn is the number of samples and ppp is the number of features. For n≫pn \gg pn≫p, this is dominated by forming the QQQ factor.

OLS is the baseline linear model — use it when features are uncorrelated and the system is well-determined (n>pn > pn>p). For ill-conditioned or underdetermined problems, prefer Ridge. For feature selection, prefer Lasso or ElasticNet. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.LinearRegression.

The predicted probability that sample xxx belongs to the positive class is given by the logistic (sigmoid) function: P(y=1∣x)=σ(w⊤x+b)=11+e−(w⊤x+b)P(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^\top x + b)}}P(y=1∣x)=σ(w⊤x+b)=1+e−(w⊤x+b)1​ Decision boundary: a sample is assigned to the positive class when P(y=1∣x)≥0.5P(y=1 \mid x) \ge 0.5P(y=1∣x)≥0.5, i.e., when w⊤x+b≥0w^\top x + b \ge 0w⊤x+b≥0.

Larger CCC means less regularization. This formulation matches scikit-learn's LogisticRegression.

Skigen uses Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (a Newton-type method) to solve the logistic regression problem. At each iteration: Compute predicted probabilities: p^i=σ(w⊤xi+b)\hat{p}_i = \sigma(w^\top x_i + b)p^​i​=σ(w⊤xi​+b) Compute the diagonal weight matrix: Sii=p^i(1−p^i)S_{ii} = \hat{p}_i(1 - \hat{p}_i)Sii​=p^​i​(1−p^​i​) Compute the working response: zi=w⊤xi+(yi−p^i)/Siiz_i = w^\top x_i + (y_i - \hat{p}_i) / S_{ii}zi​=w⊤xi​+(yi​−p^​i​)/Sii​ Solve the weighted least squares problem: w←(X⊤SX+1CI)−1X⊤Szw \leftarrow (X^\top S X + \frac{1}{C} I)^{-1} X^\top S zw←(X⊤SX+C1​I)−1X⊤Sz The algorithm uses a diagonal Hessian approximation for efficiency and converges quadratically near the optimum. The sigmoid computation is numerically stabilized to avoid overflow for large inputs.

For more than two classes, Skigen uses the One-vs-Rest (OvR) strategy: a separate binary classifier is fitted for each class against all others. The class with the highest predicted probability is selected.

MeanShift is a mode-seeking clustering algorithm. Each sample starts as a seed, repeatedly moves to the mean of nearby samples inside the bandwidth ball, and the converged seeds are merged into cluster centers. Mirrors sklearn.cluster.MeanShift for dense, brute-force flat-kernel clustering.

Evaluation metrics for quantifying model performance on regression and classification tasks.

The average of squared differences between predictions and true values. Penalizes large errors quadratically. MSE(y,y^)=1n∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2\text{MSE}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2MSE(y,y^​)=n1​i=1∑n​(yi​−y^​i​)2
The square root of MSE, expressed in the same units as the target variable. RMSE(y,y^)=MSE(y,y^)\text{RMSE}(y, \hat{y}) = \sqrt{\text{MSE}(y, \hat{y})}RMSE(y,y^​)=MSE(y,y^​)​
The average of absolute differences. Less sensitive to outliers than MSE. MAE(y,y^)=1n∑i=1n∣yi−y^i∣\text{MAE}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|MAE(y,y^​)=n1​i=1∑n​∣yi​−y^​i​∣

Measures the proportion of variance in the target explained by the model. A score of 1.01.01.0 indicates perfect prediction; 0.00.00.0 corresponds to always predicting the mean yˉ\bar{y}yˉ​. R2(y,y^)=1−∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2∑i=1n(yi−yˉ)2R^2(y, \hat{y}) = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}R2(y,y^​)=1−∑i=1n​(yi​−yˉ​)2∑i=1n​(yi​−y^​i​)2​ The score can be negative if the model is worse than predicting the mean. #include <Skigen/Metrics> Skigen::Metrics::mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::root_mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::r2_score(y_true, y_pred); Function Description mean_squared_error Mean of squared errors root_mean_squared_error Square root of MSE mean_absolute_error Mean of absolute errors r2_score Coefficient of determination

All binary classification metrics below are defined in terms of the confusion matrix entries: TP (True Positives): correctly predicted positive TN (True Negatives): correctly predicted negative FP (False Positives): negative samples incorrectly predicted as positive FN (False Negatives): positive samples incorrectly predicted as negative
Fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive. High precision means few false alarms. Precision=TPTP+FP\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}Precision=TP+FPTP​
Fraction of actual positives that are correctly identified. High recall means few missed positives. Recall=TPTP+FN\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}Recall=TP+FNTP​

The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both concerns equally. NelderMead is a derivative-free direct-search optimizer. It maintains a simplex of n + 1 vertices in an n-dimensional parameter space and updates it with reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrink steps. This is useful when gradients are unavailable, noisy, or too expensive to compute.
Skigen::NelderMead<double> optimizer( max_iter = 1000, xatol = 1e-8, fatol = 1e-8, initial_step = 0.05);
Method Description minimize(x0, objective) Build an initial simplex around x0 and minimize. minimize_simplex(simplex, objective) Start from an explicit (n + 1) x n simplex.

NelderMead::Result contains: Field Description x Best point found. fun Objective value at x. nit Iterations completed. nfev Objective evaluations. success Whether convergence tolerances were met. message Termination message. final_simplex Final simplex vertices. final_values Objective values at final vertices.

#include <Skigen/Optimization> Eigen::VectorXd x0(2); x0 << -3.0, 4.0; Skigen::NelderMead<double> optimizer(500, 1e-9, 1e-12, 0.2); auto result = optimizer.minimize(x0, [](const Eigen::VectorXd& x) { return std::pow(x(0) - 1.5, 2) + std::pow(x(1) + 2.0, 2); });

Mirrors the classical Nelder-Mead algorithm used by scipy.optimize.minimize(method="Nelder-Mead") at the algorithmic level. NuSVC is a nu-parametrised binary support vector classifier. Instead of the penalty C, it uses nu, which bounds the fraction of margin errors and support vectors.

The estimator solves the nu-SVM dual with a dedicated SMO variant. Two equality constraints — one per class on the dual sums — replace the single equality of the C-SVM. Each class is initialised to a uniform mass nu * n / 2, and optimisation works on pairs within the same class so the per-class sums are preserved under a [0, 1] box bound. The intercept is recovered from the free support vectors. The decision function is the kernel-weighted sum of dual_coef_ plus the intercept; predict returns the class with the matching sign.

Parameter Default Description nu 0.5 Upper bound on the fraction of margin errors and lower bound on the fraction of support vectors, in (0, 1]. Infeasible values throw std::invalid_argument. kernel RBF Kernel function. gamma 0 Kernel coefficient; 0 means 1 / (n_features * var).
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the nu-SVM classifier; requires exactly two classes. decision_function(X) Signed kernel scores. predict(X) Predicted class labels.
Accessor Description support() Indices of the support vectors. n_support() Number of support vectors. classes() The two class labels seen during fit. intercept() Decision intercept.

NuSVR is a nu-parametrised support vector regressor. Rather than fixing the tube width epsilon, the nu parameter controls the fraction of support vectors and the algorithm learns epsilon from the data.

The estimator runs a two-phase fit. The first pass solves the dual with epsilon = 0 to estimate the residual scale; the fitted tube width is then set to the 1 - nu quantile of the absolute residuals. The second pass re-fits with that effective epsilon. The dual is optimised by a projected sub-gradient method over the dual coefficients with a box bound derived from C. The prediction is the kernel-weighted sum of dual_coef_ plus the intercept.

Parameter Default Description nu 0.5 Upper bound on the fraction of margin errors and lower bound on the fraction of support vectors, in (0, 1]. C 1.0 Regularisation strength; the box bound on the dual coefficients. kernel RBF Kernel function. gamma 0 Kernel coefficient; 0 means 1 / (n_features * var).

OneClassSVM performs unsupervised outlier / novelty detection by fitting the one-class nu-SVM dual with a dedicated SMO solver.

The estimator solves a quadratic program over the dual coefficients with a unit sum constraint and a box bound 1 / (nu * n), then computes an offset rho from the support vectors. The decision function is the kernel-weighted sum of the dual coefficients minus rho; predict returns +1 for inliers and -1 for outliers.

Principal Component Analysis finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance in the data, enabling dimensionality reduction while retaining as much information as possible. The examples/pca_clustering_workflow.cpp program projects 10-D Gaussian clusters down to 2-D with PCA and recovers them with KMeans, rendered via SkigenPlot:

Given an n×pn \times pn×p data matrix XXX: Center the data: Xc=X−1xˉ⊤X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\bar{x}^\topXc​=X−1xˉ⊤, where xˉ\bar{x}xˉ is the column-wise mean. Compute the SVD of XcX_cXc​: Xc=UΣV⊤X_c = U \Sigma V^\topXc​=UΣV⊤. Truncate to kkk components by keeping only the first kkk columns of VVV (right singular vectors). The projection onto the kkk-dimensional subspace is: Z=Xc Vk∈Rn×kZ = X_c \, V_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}Z=Xc​Vk​∈Rn×k

The explained variance of the jjj-th component is derived from the singular values: explained_variancej=σj2n−1\text{explained\_variance}_j = \frac{\sigma_j^2}{n - 1}explained_variancej​=n−1σj2​​ using n−1n-1n−1 degrees of freedom (Bessel's correction), consistent with scikit-learn. The explained variance ratio measures the proportion of total variance captured by each component: explained_variance_ratioj=σj2∑i=1pσi2\text{explained\_variance\_ratio}_j = \frac{\sigma_j^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \sigma_i^2}explained_variance_ratioj​=∑i=1p​σi2​σj2​​

PCA always centers the data before decomposition. For data that should not be centered (e.g., sparse matrices, TF-IDF), use TruncatedSVD instead. The components are ordered by decreasing explained variance. inverse_transform reconstructs an approximation: X^=ZVk⊤+1xˉ⊤\hat{X} = Z V_k^\top + \mathbf{1}\bar{x}^\topX^=ZVk⊤​+1xˉ⊤. Skigen uses Eigen::JacobiSVD for full SVD. Mirrors sklearn.decomposition.PCA.

Perceptron is a linear classifier trained with the perceptron update rule. Skigen implements it as a thin estimator over the shared SGDClassifier training path with perceptron loss, no regularization by default, and eta0=1.

For a binary encoded target y∈{−1,1}y \in \{-1, 1\}y∈{−1,1} and margin y(wTx+b)y(w^Tx + b)y(wTx+b), the perceptron updates when the sample is misclassified: w←w+ηyx,b←b+ηyw \leftarrow w + \eta yx,\qquad b \leftarrow b + \eta yw←w+ηyx,b←b+ηy Multiclass classification uses one-vs-rest training, matching the existing Skigen SGD classifier architecture.

A compile-time pipeline that chains transformers and a final estimator into a single object. All step types are verified at compile time using C++ template metaprogramming — incompatible steps produce a compile error rather than a runtime failure.

For a pipeline with transformers T1,T2,…,TmT_1, T_2, \ldots, T_mT1​,T2​,…,Tm​ and a final estimator EEE: Fitting — each transformer is fit and transforms the data sequentially before the estimator is fit: E.fit ⁣(Tm.fit_transform ⁣(⋯T1.fit_transform(X)⋯ ), y)E.\text{fit}\!\left(T_m.\text{fit\_transform}\!\left(\cdots T_1.\text{fit\_transform}(X)\cdots\right),\; y\right)E.fit(Tm​.fit_transform(⋯T1​.fit_transform(X)⋯),y) Prediction — data passes through each transformer's transform, then the estimator's predict: y^=E.predict ⁣(Tm.transform ⁣(⋯T1.transform(X)⋯ ))\hat{y} = E.\text{predict}\!\left(T_m.\text{transform}\!\left(\cdots T_1.\text{transform}(X)\cdots\right)\right)y^​=E.predict(Tm​.transform(⋯T1​.transform(X)⋯)) This ensures that the same preprocessing applied during training is automatically applied during prediction, preventing train/test skew. Mirrors sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline.
auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline(step1, step2, ..., estimator); Or explicitly: Skigen::Pipeline<StandardScaler<>, PCA<>, LinearRegression<>> pipe(scaler, pca, model);
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit all steps sequentially predict(X) Transform through all steps, then predict score(X, y) Transform and score get<I>() Access step at index I

#include <Skigen/Pipeline> #include <Skigen/Preprocessing> #include <Skigen/LinearModel> auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline( Skigen::StandardScaler<>(), Skigen::LinearRegression<>() ); pipe.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "R²: " << pipe.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n"; // Access fitted scaler auto& scaler = pipe.get<0>(); std::cout << "Mean: " << scaler.mean() << "\n";
Generates a new feature matrix consisting of all polynomial combinations of the input features up to a given degree. This enables fitting polynomial relationships with a linear model.

For input features [x1,x2][x_1, x_2][x1​,x2​] with degree d=2d = 2d=2 and bias: ϕ(x)=[1, x1, x2, x12, x1x2, x22]\phi(x) = [1,\; x_1,\; x_2,\; x_1^2,\; x_1 x_2,\; x_2^2]ϕ(x)=[1,x1​,x2​,x12​,x1​x2​,x22​] The number of output features is: output features=(p+dd)(with bias),(p+dd)−1(without bias)\text{output features} = \binom{p + d}{d} \quad \text{(with bias)}, \qquad \binom{p + d}{d} - 1 \quad \text{(without bias)}output features=(dp+d​)(with bias),(dp+d​)−1(without bias) where ppp is the number of input features and ddd is the degree. For example, p=2, d=3p = 2,\, d = 3p=2,d=3 produces (53)=10\binom{5}{3} = 10(35​)=10 features.

When interaction_only = true, only interaction features are generated (no powers xjkx_j^kxjk​ for k>1k > 1k>1). For [x1,x2][x_1, x_2][x1​,x2​] with d=2d = 2d=2, this yields [1, x1, x2, x1x2][1,\; x_1,\; x_2,\; x_1 x_2][1,x1​,x2​,x1​x2​].

Polynomial features combined with a linear model create a polynomial regression pipeline: auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline( Skigen::PolynomialFeatures<>(3), Skigen::LinearRegression<>() ); This fits y=w0+w1x+w2x2+w3x3y = w_0 + w_1 x + w_2 x^2 + w_3 x^3y=w0​+w1​x+w2​x2+w3​x3 while keeping the model linear in the parameters. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.PolynomialFeatures.

Radius-neighbor estimators use all training samples within a fixed Euclidean radius of each query sample. Skigen v1.2.0 includes dense brute-force implementations of: RadiusNeighborsClassifier, which predicts by majority vote among neighbors inside the radius. RadiusNeighborsRegressor, which predicts the mean target value of neighbors inside the radius. If a query sample has no neighbors inside the radius, prediction throws so the caller can choose a larger radius or handle the outlier explicitly.

Method Description fit(X, y) Store training samples and targets. predict(X) Predict labels or target values from radius neighbors. radius_neighbors(X, radius) Return sorted training indices inside the radius.

Mirrors the dense brute-force subset of sklearn.neighbors.RadiusNeighborsClassifier and sklearn.neighbors.RadiusNeighborsRegressor. Distance weighting, custom outlier labels, custom metrics, sparse input, parallelism, and graph-return helpers are deferred. API Reference For full signatures see the RadiusNeighborsClassifier API Reference and RadiusNeighborsRegressor API Reference.
An ensemble of decision trees, each grown on a bootstrap sample of the data and a random subspace of features. Class predictions are formed by majority (hard) or averaged-probability (soft) voting.

Each tree is fit independently on a bootstrap resample; at every split a random subset of max_features candidate features is considered. Decorrelating the trees this way reduces the variance of the averaged ensemble without materially increasing bias. When oob_score=true, out-of-bag samples (those not drawn for a given tree) provide an unbiased generalisation estimate for free.
Skigen::RandomForestClassifier<Scalar> model(int n_estimators = 100, CriterionClf = Gini, std::optional<int> max_depth = nullopt, ...);

Accessor Description estimators() The fitted trees. feature_importances() Mean impurity decrease per feature. oob_score() Out-of-bag accuracy (when enabled).

A bagged ensemble of regression trees whose predictions are averaged. Trades a small bias increase for a large variance reduction relative to a single deep tree.

Recursive Feature Elimination: repeatedly fit an estimator and discard the weakest feature until the target count remains.

Ridge regression addresses the instability of Ordinary Least Squares when features are correlated or when the design matrix is near-singular. It does so by adding an ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ penalty on the coefficient vector, shrinking all coefficients toward zero and improving the conditioning of the problem.

min⁡w∥Xw−y∥22+α∥w∥22\min_w \|Xw - y\|_2^2 + \alpha \|w\|_2^2wmin​∥Xw−y∥22​+α∥w∥22​ where α≥0\alpha \ge 0α≥0 controls the regularization strength: larger α\alphaα increases shrinkage and reduces variance at the cost of higher bias. This formulation matches scikit-learn's Ridge, which minimizes ∥y−Xw∥22+α∥w∥22\|y - Xw\|_2^2 + \alpha\|w\|_2^2∥y−Xw∥22​+α∥w∥22​.

Setting the gradient to zero yields the normal equation: (X⊤X+αI) w=X⊤y(X^\top X + \alpha I)\, w = X^\top y(X⊤X+αI)w=X⊤y Skigen solves this system via Cholesky decomposition (Eigen::LLT), which exploits the fact that X⊤X+αIX^\top X + \alpha IX⊤X+αI is symmetric positive definite for α>0\alpha > 0α>0. The solution is: w^=(X⊤X+αI)−1X⊤y\hat{w} = (X^\top X + \alpha I)^{-1} X^\top yw^=(X⊤X+αI)−1X⊤y When fit_intercept is enabled, the data is centered before fitting: Xc=X−1xˉ⊤X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\bar{x}^\topXc​=X−1xˉ⊤ and yc=y−yˉy_c = y - \bar{y}yc​=y−yˉ​. The intercept is then recovered as b=yˉ−xˉ⊤w^b = \bar{y} - \bar{x}^\top \hat{w}b=yˉ​−xˉ⊤w^.
Forming X⊤XX^\top XX⊤X costs O(np2)O(n p^2)O(np2), and the Cholesky solve costs O(p3)O(p^3)O(p3), giving an overall complexity of O(np2+p3)O(n p^2 + p^3)O(np2+p3) — the same order as OLS.

Multicollinearity: When features are correlated and OLS produces unstable estimates with large variance. Ill-conditioned problems: When X⊤XX^\top XX⊤X is near-singular, Ridge stabilizes the solution by shifting eigenvalues away from zero. ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ preference: When all features are expected to contribute and full sparsity (zeroing coefficients) is not desired — use Lasso or ElasticNet for that.

RidgeClassifier turns classification into ridge regression on encoded class targets. For binary classification, targets are encoded as -1 for the first sorted class and +1 for the second sorted class. For multiclass classification, Skigen fits one one-vs-rest ridge target per class and predicts with the largest decision score.
The dense solver fits: min⁡W∥Y−XW∥22+α∥W∥22\min_W \|Y - XW\|_2^2 + \alpha \|W\|_2^2Wmin​∥Y−XW∥22​+α∥W∥22​ where Y is the encoded class target matrix.

Method Description fit(X, y) Fit ridge coefficients on encoded class targets. predict(X) Predict original integer class labels. decision_function(X) Return binary or one-vs-rest scores. score(X, y) Return mean accuracy.
Accessor Description classes() Sorted original class labels. coef() Binary shape (1, n_features), multiclass shape (n_classes, n_features). intercept() Binary shape (1), multiclass shape (n_classes).
#include <Skigen/LinearModel> Skigen::RidgeClassifier<double> clf(1.0); clf.fit(X, y); Eigen::VectorXi pred = clf.predict(X_test);

Centers and scales features using statistics that are robust to outliers: the median for centering and the interquartile range (IQR) for scaling.

Zij=Xij−medianjIQRjZ_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij} - \text{median}_j}{\text{IQR}_j}Zij​=IQRj​Xij​−medianj​​ where: medianj\text{median}_jmedianj​ is the median of feature jjj IQRj=Q75(Xj)−Q25(Xj)\text{IQR}_j = Q_{75}(X_j) - Q_{25}(X_j)IQRj​=Q75​(Xj​)−Q25​(Xj​) is the interquartile range Unlike StandardScaler, the median and IQR are not influenced by extreme values, so the scaling remains stable even with outliers in the data.

Data with outliers: When the data contains extreme values that would distort mean/variance-based scaling. Robust pipelines: As a preprocessing step before models sensitive to feature scales. The quantile_range parameter allows customizing the centering quantiles (e.g., using the 5th and 95th percentiles). Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.RobustScaler.

Linear models fitted via Stochastic Gradient Descent, well-suited for large-scale learning where batch solvers are prohibitively expensive. SGD processes one sample at a time, updating the weight vector with a noisy gradient estimate.

Hinge loss (linear SVM) — for maximum-margin classification with labels yi∈{−1,+1}y_i \in \{-1, +1\}yi​∈{−1,+1}: Li=max⁡(0, 1−yi w⊤xi)L_i = \max(0,\; 1 - y_i \, w^\top x_i)Li​=max(0,1−yi​w⊤xi​) Log loss (logistic regression) — for probabilistic binary classification with labels yi∈{0,1}y_i \in \{0, 1\}yi​∈{0,1}: Li=−[yilog⁡σ(w⊤xi)+(1−yi)log⁡(1−σ(w⊤xi))]L_i = -\left[ y_i \log \sigma(w^\top x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \sigma(w^\top x_i)) \right]Li​=−[yi​logσ(w⊤xi​)+(1−yi​)log(1−σ(w⊤xi​))] where σ(z)=1/(1+e−z)\sigma(z) = 1/(1 + e^{-z})σ(z)=1/(1+e−z) is the sigmoid function.
Squared error loss: Li=12(yi−w⊤xi)2L_i = \frac{1}{2}(y_i - w^\top x_i)^2Li​=21​(yi​−w⊤xi​)2

For each randomly selected sample (xi,yi)(x_i, y_i)(xi​,yi​), the weight vector is updated as: w←w−ηt(∇wLi+α w)w \leftarrow w - \eta_t \left( \nabla_w L_i + \alpha\, w \right)w←w−ηt​(∇w​Li​+αw) where α w\alpha\, wαw is the ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ regularization gradient and ηt\eta_tηt​ is the learning rate at step ttt.

Skigen uses an inverse scaling schedule matching scikit-learn's "invscaling": ηt=η01+η0⋅α⋅t\eta_t = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \eta_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot t}ηt​=1+η0​⋅α⋅tη0​​ This schedule gradually decreases the step size, ensuring convergence to the optimum.

Training runs for at most max_iter epochs. At the end of each epoch, convergence is checked: training stops when the loss improvement drops below tol. Shuffling the data each epoch is recommended for faster convergence. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier and SGDRegressor.
